nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef crests and deeper reefs adjacent to steep slopes and drop-offs, at depths of roughly 10–50 m

diet.notes: The only true zooplanktivore genus among the angelfishes. Has been documented feeding on zooplankton in midwater aggregations well above the reef. In aquariums, generally advised to offer a varied diet including Spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations, and mysis or frozen shrimp.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5701x3800. Tack-sharp in-situ male: distinctive lyretail with long trailing filaments and dark margins, yellow nape spot, bold black striping eye to tail; fills the frame, striking. Recommended.
Rickard Zerpe, 4608x3456. Sharp clean lateral female in-situ, silvery-gray body with straight bold black stripes and freckled lyretail; shows the female form for sexual-dimorphism contrast.
Diego Delso, 5393x3595. Sharp male in open blue water, full lyretail with long filaments; clean background makes the fish pop, adds variety of setting.

summary: Lamarck's Angelfish is a sexually dimorphic, peaceful planktivore from the Indo-West Pacific, widely considered one of the more reef-safe angelfish in the hobby and generally recommended for larger reef aquariums of at least 492 litres.
careNotes:
Lamarck's Angelfish (Genicanthus lamarck) stands apart from most other marine angelfish because it belongs to the only zooplanktivorous genus within the family Pomacanthidae — meaning it feeds on tiny drifting animals called zooplankton rather than sponges or corals. This feeding habit is why it is widely considered safer around corals and invertebrates than many other angelfish in the hobby.

Both sexes share a silvery-gray body with bold horizontal black stripes running from the eye to the tail; females tend to have more pronounced striping, with one stripe extending the full length of the caudal fin, while males develop a distinctive lyretail caudal fin with black margins. The difference in appearance between the sexes makes the species one of the more visually straightforward angelfish to sex at a glance.

In the wild, Lamarck's Angelfish tends to form small haremic groups consisting of one dominant male and 2–6 females. Only one male per aquarium is generally recommended, as males have been reported to be aggressive toward one another and toward other male Genicanthus. The species has been reported to occasionally chase small planktivores such as anthias and small wrasses, but generally tends to leave other tank inhabitants alone.

Compared to many other large angelfish, Lamarck's Angelfish is commonly considered more straightforward to feed in captivity, as its natural planktivorous diet translates well to prepared and frozen foods.
breedingNotes: Lamarck's Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning females are capable of changing sex to become males when the dominant male in the group is absent. In the wild, the species typically lives in haremic groups consisting of one male and several females. Captive breeding has been reported but remains uncommon in the hobby. The species tends to spawn in open water, releasing pelagic eggs and larvae that have proven difficult to rear to juvenile stage.

additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), adult max size 25 cm, native range from Gulf of Thailand to Solomon Islands, depth 10–50 m. Used as a tertiary cross-check for size and taxonomy.

fish.breedingNotes: Lamarck's Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite; females can change sex to become males when the dominant male is absent. Captive breeding has been reported but remains uncommon. The species spawns in open water, releasing pelagic larvae that are difficult to rear.
